Name: pacbayeslatencyinference
Title: PAC-Bayes Frameworks for Latency-Critical Inference in Machine Learning
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that PAC-Bayes bounds can be leveraged to derive latency-critical inference protocols that ensure strict wall-clock budgets while maintaining high accuracy in machine learning models.
Related Work: Existing literature on PAC-Bayes frameworks primarily focuses on theoretical guarantees for model generalization and robustness. Recent work has explored the application of PAC-Bayes bounds to neural network training, but there is limited research on their application to latency-critical inference scenarios. This proposal distinguishes itself by explicitly targeting real-time inference with strict latency constraints, using PAC-Bayes bounds to derive and enforce these constraints.
Abstract: This proposal explores the application of PAC-Bayes frameworks to derive latency-critical inference protocols in machine learning. We hypothesize that PAC-Bayes bounds can provide a theoretical foundation for ensuring strict wall-clock budgets while maintaining high accuracy. Our approach involves developing a Jacobian-based layer-skipping mechanism to minimize hidden-state perturbation and enforce latency constraints. We will empirically validate our hypothesis through a series of experiments, including latency-accuracy trade-off analysis and robustness evaluation under structured perturbations. The proposed method aims to deliver a computationally efficient and theoretically grounded solution for real-time inference, with applications in latency-sensitive domains such as autonomous systems and real-time analytics.
Experiments: Latency-Accuracy Trade-Off Analysis: We will train a set of neural networks on a standard dataset (e.g., CIFAR-10) and apply our PAC-Bayes-based layer-skipping mechanism. We will measure the inference latency and accuracy, plotting the Pareto frontiers to analyze the trade-offs.

Stability and Robustness Evaluation: We will evaluate the stability of our method under structured perturbations and domain shifts. Specifically, we will introduce input noise and domain shifts to assess the robustness of our latency-critical inference protocols.

Comparison with Baselines: We will compare our method with existing latency-critical inference techniques, such as early exit and dynamic inference, to demonstrate the effectiveness of our PAC-Bayes-based approach.
Scalability and Resource Utilization: We will measure the resource utilization (e.g., memory, FLOPs) of our method on different hardware configurations to ensure it is compute-frugal and scalable.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Complexity of PAC-Bayes Bounds: Deriving and applying PAC-Bayes bounds in a real-time inference setting can be complex and may require significant theoretical and computational effort.

Generalization to Different Architectures: The effectiveness of our layer-skipping mechanism may vary across different neural network architectures, and extensive experimentation will be needed to establish its generalizability.
Latency Constraints: Ensuring strict wall-clock budgets in real-time systems can be challenging, and our method may need to be fine-tuned to meet these constraints in practice.
